metadata.title: Logo: 4–5 Concepts + the Rationale
metadata.playlist: Make It Visible — Visual Identity & Wireframes
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 7 (Visual) brief — logo concepts and rationale; due 2026-07-17

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam, Episode 2 of 6. The creative brief is done. You have six fields: name, description, audience, adjectives, color direction, inspiration brands. Now we turn those six fields into a visual object — a logo. This episode covers the five logo concept types, how to evaluate them against your brief, and how to write a rationale that justifies the choice you make. The output of this episode is one selected logo and a one-page rationale. You do not need design software for this episode — you need nina and your brief.

props.command: I have my creative brief. Generate 4–5 logo concepts, run the three technical tests, and write a selection rationale — one selected logo, one page of reasoning.
props.runningText: 5 concept types · 3 technical tests (favicon, BW, SVG) · 4-part rationale · 1 selection…
output: 5 logo types: wordmark · lettermark · icon+wordmark · abstract · emblem
3 technical tests every concept must pass before selection
4-part rationale: type · adjective match · color+type story · 1 honest limitation

beats.narration_text: Five logo concept types. Wordmark: the brand name in a custom typeface. Nothing else. Works best when the name is short and distinctive. Lettermark: initials only, in a styled typeface. Works when the name is long or hard to abbreviate visually. Icon plus wordmark: a graphic mark next to the name. The most flexible format — the icon can be used alone at small sizes, the full lockup at large sizes. Abstract mark: a geometric or abstract graphic with no literal meaning. Requires brand recognition to work, which is why it is usually not right for a student project launching from zero. Emblem: name inside a shape, like a seal or badge. Classic and authoritative, but does not scale down well. For a student product with no existing brand recognition, icon plus wordmark is almost always the right starting point — the icon gives visual memory, the name gives immediate context.

props.artifactHeading: Pick the type before generating — it shapes the brief
artifactLines: Wordmark: brand name in custom type. Best when name is short + distinctive.
Lettermark: initials only. Best when name is long or visually hard to abbreviate.
Icon + wordmark: graphic mark beside the name. Most flexible — icon alone at small sizes.
Abstract mark: geometric/abstract, no literal meaning. Needs brand recognition — not ideal from zero.
Emblem: name inside a shape/badge. Authoritative, but won't scale to favicon.
Default for new student products: icon + wordmark.
props.sparkLine: Icon + wordmark: the icon gives visual memory, the name gives immediate context.

beats.narration_text: Three technical tests before you commit to a concept. Test one: the favicon. Does the logo read at 32 by 32 pixels? If the concept has fine detail — thin letterforms, complex icon — it will blur into noise at small sizes. Test two: black and white. Does the logo work without color? A logo that requires color to be legible has a structural problem. Every logo needs a one-color version that works on a white background and a white version that works on a dark background. Test three: SVG. Can the logo be drawn as a vector — clean geometric shapes with no gradients or textures? SVG is the delivery format for web and print; a raster-only logo is not a logo for production. Run all three tests as you evaluate nina's concept outputs. A concept that fails any one of these is eliminated before selection.

props.artifactHeading: Fail any one of these = not a production logo
artifactLines: Test 1 — Favicon: does it read at 32×32px? Fine detail and thin strokes fail this.
Test 2 — Black + white: does it work without color? If color is structural, it has a problem.
Test 3 — SVG: can it be drawn as clean vectors (no gradients, no textures)?
Run all three before selection. Eliminate any concept that fails even one.
These aren't style preferences — they're production requirements.
props.sparkLine: A logo that only works in color, or only at large sizes, isn't a production logo.

beats.narration_text: The rationale document is your written justification for the logo you chose. It has four parts. Part one: which concept you selected and which type it is. Part two: how the concept connects to each of the brief's adjectives — one sentence per adjective. If your brief said 'calm, precise, approachable,' the rationale explains how the chosen logo delivers all three. Part three: the color and typography story — which colors from field five the logo activates and which font from the brief's inspiration it most closely resembles. Part four: one honest limitation — where this logo might not work as designed. A strong rationale names the limitation before the grader finds it. The rationale is also how you demonstrate to nina that your selection is intentional, not arbitrary — paste the rationale back into the next nina session as context for the mood board and style guide.

props.artifactHeading: Written justification for the logo you chose
artifactLines: 1. Selected concept + type (wordmark, icon+wordmark, etc.).
2. Adjective connection — one sentence per adjective from field 4 of the brief.
3. Color + type story — which direction colors activate, which inspiration font it resembles.
4. One honest limitation — where this concept won't work.
Name the limitation before the grader finds it. Then paste the rationale into the next nina session.
props.sparkLine: The rationale proves your selection was intentional. Paste it as context for the style guide.

beats.narration_text: Selecting one from five is the decision point in this episode, and it is a judgment call that nina cannot make for you. Run all five concepts through the three technical tests. Eliminate any that fail favicon, black-and-white, or SVG. From the ones that survive, ask: which concept most directly embodies the three adjectives from my brief? If two concepts tie, the tiebreaker is specificity — the concept that is more specific to your product's domain wins. An icon that could belong to any tech startup is weaker than an icon that only makes sense for your product. The plus-one principle applies here: after nina generates concepts, you ask 'does any of these actually look like the brand I described in my brief?' If the answer is no for all five, the brief inputs were not specific enough. Rewrite the brief and run again before selecting.

props.artifactHeading: Technical tests eliminate. Brief alignment selects.
artifactLines: Step 1: run all 5 concepts through favicon / BW / SVG. Eliminate failures.
Step 2: which surviving concept most directly embodies the 3 adjectives?
Tiebreaker: specificity. Generic tech-startup icon loses to domain-specific icon.
+1 question: 'Does any of these actually look like the brand I described in my brief?'
If no for all 5 → brief inputs were underspecified. Rewrite before selecting.
props.sparkLine: Generic loses to specific every time. Domain-specific icon beats any generic mark.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. Five logo types: wordmark, lettermark, icon plus wordmark, abstract mark, emblem. Three technical tests: favicon, black and white, SVG. One rationale document: concept type, connection to adjectives, color and type story, one honest limitation. The deliverable is one selected logo and a completed rationale. The selection is the human decision; the generation is nina's job. Do not move to Episode 3 without a selected concept — the mood board and style guide in the next episode will be built around the logo you chose here.

props.artifactHeading: One selection + one rationale — human decision, nina generation
artifactLines: 5 logo types · 3 technical tests (favicon / BW / SVG) · 4-part rationale.
Deliverable: 1 selected logo + completed rationale document.
Selection = human judgment. Generation = nina's job.
Do not move to Episode 3 without a selected concept.
The mood board and style guide are built around the logo chosen here.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the nina logo prompt. Paste it with your completed creative brief in context. The prompt reads: Here is my creative brief: [paste the brief]. Generate 4 to 5 distinct logo concepts. For each concept: name the logo type — wordmark, lettermark, icon plus wordmark, abstract mark, or emblem. Describe the visual approach in two sentences — what the mark looks like and why it fits the brief's adjectives. Note one limitation — where this concept might not work technically or contextually. After the five concepts, add a Selection Recommendation: which concept best satisfies the brief and why, in two sentences. After running the prompt: run the three technical tests — favicon, black and white, SVG — on the recommended concept. If it passes all three, that is your selected logo. Write the four-part rationale and keep it for Episode 3.

props.command: Here is my creative brief: [paste brief]. Generate 4–5 distinct logo concepts. For each: name the type (wordmark / lettermark / icon+wordmark / abstract / emblem), describe the visual approach in 2 sentences, note 1 limitation. After the 5 concepts: add a Selection Recommendation — which concept best satisfies the brief and why, in 2 sentences.
props.runningText: after the prompt: run favicon / BW / SVG tests on the recommended concept — if it passes all 3, that's your logo. Write the 4-part rationale before Episode 3…

beats.narration_text: Logo: 4–5 Concepts + the Rationale. Liam, in for Bear.
